Key Talking Points — Class Imbalance & Augmentation

1. Why class imbalance is a core problem

Out of ~5 700 confirmed exoplanets, only ~60 fall into the habitable zone by standard criteria (radius 0.5–2.5 R⊕, stellar flux 0.2–2.0 S⊕, T_eff 2500–7000 K). That is roughly a 1:95 imbalance ratio. Any supervised model trained on raw counts will learn to predict "not habitable" almost every time and still achieve >98% accuracy — a classic accuracy paradox.

2. Why augmentation is hard for exoplanet data

  • Multivariate coupling: Planet parameters are not independent — radius, mass, insolation, and orbital period are linked by Kepler's third law, the mass–radius relation, and stellar luminosity. Naïve oversampling (e.g., SMOTE) ignores these astrophysical constraints and can produce planets that violate conservation laws.
  • Small minority class: With only ~60 real habitable worlds, the CTGAN's generator has very few exemplars to learn from, increasing the risk of mode collapse (generating near-identical copies of the training set).
  • Right-skewed features: Parameters like mass_earth and period_days span orders of magnitude. Without log-transforms, the GAN's internal Gaussian mixtures cannot represent the long tails, producing physically absurd outputs.

3. How we safeguard against nonsense

  1. Log-transform before training: We apply log1p to right-skewed columns (mass, period, semi-major axis, stellar parameters) before CTGAN training and reverse with expm1 after sampling.
  2. Hard physics filter: Every synthetic planet passes through validate_synthetic_data() which enforces absolute physical bounds (e.g., radius 0.3–25 R⊕, T_eq 10–4000 K, period > 0.1 d).
  3. Percentile clipping: After physics filtering, we clip each column to its 1st–99th percentile range to remove long-tail outliers that passed the coarse filter.
  4. Post-hoc habitable envelope: For conditional sampling (habitable=1), we further restrict synthetic planets to lie within the 1–99% quantile envelope of real habitable planets per feature.
  5. DCR memorization check: We compute Distance to Closest Record (synth→real vs real↔real LOO) to verify the generator is not simply memorizing training rows.
  6. Clear labeling: All synthetic data is explicitly marked as exploratory in the UI — never presented as real discoveries.
